entry.process: US-flagged boat returning from abroad: report arrival to CBP immediately on reaching US waters — the CBP ROAM app lets you clear from the cockpit in most cases; everyone stays aboard until released. Foreign-flagged: report + present in person at a port of entry.
entry.permit: US boats need no domestic cruising permit; vessels 30 ft+ pay the ~$27.50/yr DTOPS user-fee decal for customs reentries. Foreign-flagged pleasure vessels get a cruising license (~$37, up to 1 year) at the first port of entry.
entry.max_stay: Citizens unlimited. Foreign crew arriving on a private vessel need a B-1/B-2 visa — ESTA visa-waiver does NOT cover private-boat arrivals; typically admitted up to 6 months.

fishing: Licenses are per-state (e.g. Florida saltwater license; some out-of-state boats need their own). Targeting tuna, billfish or swordfish offshore requires the federal HMS Angling permit (~$26/yr). Spearfishing legal in most states with local rules — FL: no spearing within 100 yds of public beaches or bridges. FL lobster: 2-day mini-season late July, regular season Aug 6 – Mar 31.
notes: CBP ROAM app is the shortcut — file your reentry from the anchorage instead of taxiing to an office.
VHF 16 monitored coast-wide; US Coast Guard SAR is the best in the world.
No-discharge zones are common (FL Keys, Chesapeake, much of New England) — use pumpouts.
Parts of Florida restrict anchoring near residential docks; mooring fields fill fast in the Keys.
Hurricane season Jun–Nov for the Gulf and East Coast — same insurance-box rules as the Bahamas.
